full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For the longest time, the Book of Common Prayer in the United States and in England did not contain any prayers for the departed.<|10.18|> <|11.30|> And there were a lot of explanations for this omission for most people.<|17.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For most Episcopalians, prayers for the departed smacked of medieval superstition.<|7.14|> <|7.82|> So what the prayer book did, which makes some kind of sense if that's your view, is just omit anything that sounded like prayers for souls in purgatory or masses for the dead or<|18.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> all of which were common on the eve of the Protestant Reformation,<|4.02|> <|4.46|> which greatly shaped the Episcopal and Anglican tradition.<|8.64|> <|9.76|> There were also philosophical reservations for such prayers.<|13.38|> <|14.60|> What happens beyond death is both inconceivable and indescribable,<|20.28|> <|20.28|> and it was thought, therefore, that prayers for the departed just went too far.<|24.44|> <|24.96|> Right?<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.72|> wandering into places where angels fear to tread, so to speak.<|4.54|> <|6.46|> All of that changed after World War I and following the influenza epidemic of 1918.<|16.04|> <|16.80|> This Sunday, of course, is the 100th anniversary of the armistice that ended in<|21.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The war to end all wars.<|2.18|> <|2.92|> A war that's often remembered in terms of numbers that are literally inconceivable.<|7.98|> <|9.28|> 70 million military personnel deployed.<|12.10|> <|13.18|> And when you count civilians, a war that resulted in more than 20 million casualties and more than 18 million deaths.<|20.60|> <|21.10|> Wow.<|21.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So if the Episcopal Church and the Church of England were to mourn and lament and never find some measure of healing, we had to pray for our dead.<|11.32|> <|12.14|> Therefore, as so often happens, especially in the Episcopal Church, culture happens.<|17.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The world around and within us, culture reshaped liturgy.<|4.30|> <|5.38|> And this particular prayer for the dead was added to the 1928 Book of Common Prayer.<|10.72|> <|11.68|> And it was embraced, a prayer that many of us know and love forever.<|16.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we also bless, we pray, and we also bless thy holy name for all thy servants departed in this life in thy faith and fear,<|8.48|> <|9.66|> beseeching me to grant them continual growth in thy love and service.<|14.34|> <|15.34|> The first petition for the departed officially in a prayer book and one that's still in today.<|20.40|> <|20.82|> And it led, of course, to countless other prayers that we know and love for the departed.<|26.08|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.50|> So on this Sunday, we pray especially that God will grant to all who died in World War I<|6.10|> <|6.10|> and to all who have died heaven itself.<|21.68|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> of<|1.92|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> The fact that culture and historical events such as these changed how we pray is a poignant reminder that the Episcopal Church's prayers at their best are prayers for and of the world.<|19.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We want our prayers, both the official ones that we say in the book and the unofficial ones that you say in the privacy of your own heart and mind in the pews or wherever you are.<|10.70|> <|11.54|> We want all of those prayers to be in a word resonant.<|14.82|> <|15.98|> We want our prayers to resonate with what the world longs to say to God.<|22.52|> <|23.70|> Amen.<|24.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.32|> There's that wonderful folk myth that comes to mind about how you can hear the ocean in a seashell.<|12.08|> <|14.38|> The beach is not close to any of us in this moment, so you might not know this myth.<|18.88|> <|21.38|> But<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and there's snow on the ground, and it's November the 11th,<|3.68|> <|3.68|> but I will not get distracted by that.<|5.72|> <|9.04|> The myth, of course, is you hold the seashell to your ear,<|13.94|> <|13.94|> and you can explanation for that myth, and it's literally called seashell resonance.<|7.96|> <|9.72|> What we hear in the seashell, sadly, is not the ocean resonance.<|15.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> but instead is a resonance of airflow and the surrounding noise in the environment.<|6.52|> <|6.52|> And that explains why when you're at the beach, versa.<|22.52|> <|23.52|> In the deepest sense, the prayers of the church are the prayers of the world.<|28.48|> <|28.48|> The prayers of the world are the prayers of the world.<|29.20|>
